h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

failures.anchor_quote: [slides the arrow key next to the marked worksheet] On the key, grass -> rabbit means energy goes from food to eater, so rabbit -> hawk is hawk eating rabbit, not chasing.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I can put 2 in both of these, but then it looks like I missed the point of having two labels. Area scale is 4 because it counts squares, right?
scored.naiveTrajectory: The exchange was heading toward accepting the labels as sufficient to distinguish the two 2s while the learner continues to dislike the visual ambiguity.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The cover-and-uncover move is done. It proved the bare 2s are ambiguous; it does not prove the worksheet can explain itself without the drawing. Now make the row pass a stranger test. Write one short tag under each number.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: When L2 still dislikes the identical blanks, the tutor breaks from the cover-and-uncover habit and invents a new 'stranger test' task with torn paper strips and covered drawing, shifting the representation from diagram-dependence to tag-carried meaning.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The covered row exposed the ambiguity; it does not yet prove the row survives a careless swap. Run the exit gate: read it as a stranger’s worksheet. If the tags still force one side, 3 to 6; perimeter total, 12 to 24; and area scale, 2 across times 2 up equals 4, stamp it done.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After the learner's reframed pivot in L3, the tutor does not simply praise but escalates the framing into an 'exit gate' swap test, directly building on the learner's revised concern for the worksheet's independence from the drawing.

If the tags still force one side, 3 to 6; perimeter total, 12 to 24; and area scale, 2 across times 2 up equals 4, stamp it done.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: After the learner's reframed pivot in L3, the tutor does not simply praise but escalates the framing into an 'exit gate' swap test, directly building on the learner's revised concern for the worksheet's independence from the drawing.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: When L2 still dislikes the identical blanks, the tutor breaks from the cover-and-uncover habit and invents a new 'stranger test' task with torn paper strips and covered drawing, shifting the representation from diagram-dependence to tag-carried meaning.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: The cover-and-uncover move is done. It proved the bare 2s are ambiguous; it does not prove the worksheet can explain itself without the drawing. Now make the row pass a stranger test. Write one short tag under each number.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: When L2 still dislikes the identical blanks, the tutor breaks from the cover-and-uncover habit and invents a new 'stranger test' task with torn paper strips and covered drawing, shifting the representation from diagram-dependence to tag-carried meaning.

scored.recoheredEarlier: This is double the side, so I still want to call it double.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: The old word was doing both jobs: side and area. It hid the second direction.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to struggle with the conflict between wanting to call it double and the grid's correction, possibly still feeling wrong.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Keep the two columns. The old word was trying to do both jobs; the grid will not allow it.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor introduces a two-column representation to separate side and area factors, directly addressing the learner's conflation.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Keep the two columns. The old word was trying to do both jobs; the grid will not allow it.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor acknowledges the learner's separation of side and area by introducing distinct columns, reinforcing the re-framing.
